text: This is a case of a thirty-four-year-old male with an acute cholecystitis. He had an ultrasound which showed a three centimeter stone impacted in the neck of the gallbladder. He was in excruciating pain. You can see why here.
The surgeon observes pericholecystic edema and fluid around the gallbladder, with the infundibulum appearing adhered to surrounding tissues, indicating difficulty in grasping it.
The surgeon uses small hooks on the backside of the infundibulum to loosen it, improving visibility of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon confirms the identification of the cystic artery on the right and the cystic duct on the left using additional ICG dye for enhanced visualization.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to illuminate and confirm the cystic artery and cystic duct, achieving the critical view of safety. The triangle of Calot is clearly visible.
The surgeon performs a quick check for the posterior artery, which remains slightly illuminated by the ICG dye, confirming its location.
